title: About WGRA Notes
text:
WGRA (“Wiki-Grounded Research Agent”) is a pattern for research-group memory: an LLM-authored wiki that sits between a group’s raw sources and its AI coding agent (Claude Code, Cursor, and the like), acting as durable, shared, git-versioned project memory. It is built on the llm-wiki-memory-template.
These Notes are the dated companion to the main WGRA site. The main site carries the evergreen explainers and case studies; the Notes carry the field record: experiment results, design decisions, and lessons drawn from the project’s own working log, plus longer essays on arcs the deep dives do not yet cover.
Two genres:

Field notes turn the wiki’s internal log outward, one dated post at a time.
Essays develop the Research Agents strategy and its retrieval foundations.

Every post declares whether the thing it describes is built, partly built, a design, or a plan, so present tense always means it runs today.
Maintained by Chris Sweet, Center for Research Computing, University of Notre Dame (csweet1@nd.edu).

title: Why random-walk beats SPARQL for question answering
text:
NoteStatus



Argument. Grounded in observational evidence. The question-mix estimate below is informal, not a user study.


Thesis. For question answering over an LLM-maintained wiki, a random walk fits better than SPARQL. SPARQL keeps a job, but it is a maintenance job, not the primary query path.
SPARQL is a decision tree. It returns exact matches for a pattern, and if the graph does not contain the pattern, it returns empty. That is a hard failure, and it is common, because typed edges are always sparser than the relationships they encode: the extends: you need for your seed page may simply never have been written. Empty results give the model nothing to reason with.
A random walk with restart is the opposite shape. It spreads probability mass across the whole graph from the question’s seed nodes, so a sparse or provisional edge changes where the mass settles rather than breaking anything. What comes back is a soft, ranked list of neighbors, which is exactly the input a language model is good at using.
Which one you want depends on the questions. An informal estimate, drawn from two lab use cases and inspection of a real construction workflow, and explicitly not from a formal user study, puts the mix at roughly 85 percent content questions, 10 percent hybrid, and 5 percent pure topology. Content questions want soft ranking. That is most of the traffic.
SPARQL’s real band is the other 5 percent, and it is not per-query work: orphan detection, coverage checks, hub identification, extension-chain audits. Those run periodically over the graph during maintenance, where a crisp empty-or-exact answer is a feature, not a failure.
So the architecture is: random walk as the primary retrieval primitive, text search alongside it, SPARQL during audits. The graph should be built to be walkable, not rigidly typed. And in the interest of honesty, an earlier draft of this argument tried to keep SPARQL as a secondary retrieval tool; that was tool-fitted reasoning, and it was dropped.

title: The same walk at two scales
text:
NoteStatus



Design. The corpus side is demonstrated; the wiki side is unbuilt, with several open design questions.


Thesis. One random-walk-with-restart primitive runs at two scales. The math is identical; only the graph and the seeding change.
At the wiki scale, the nodes are pages plus lightweight content nodes, and the edges are structural (frontmatter edges, wikilinks materialized as weighted mentions, inverses) and content-derived. Seed the walk from the concepts in a question, let it settle, and the top pages become a reading list, surfacing pages that name-matching would miss. At the corpus scale, the nodes are chunks, entities, and documents, and the edges are containment, chunk-mentions-entity, citation, and cooccurrence. Seed from the question’s entities and the top chunks come back.
Why the same code serves both: a random walk with restart is a fixed-point iteration on a stochastic matrix. It cares about dimensionality, connectivity, the seed distribution, and the restart probability. It does not care whether a node is a page or a chunk. So one implementation, a graph loader, a seed extractor, a walk runner, a ranker, and a presenter, covers both layers, and on the wiki side the whole thing fits in a few hundred lines of Python.
The two layers compose in either order. Wiki first, then corpus: recall from durable memory, and drop down to the raw corpus for evidence the wiki has not yet cited. Corpus first, then wiki assembly: pull the chunks, then synthesize a new page and file it back into memory.
What is honestly unsettled: the wiki side has not been built. The restart probability needs tuning, the edge-weight schema is unfixed (the experiments have not been run), and a single joint wiki-plus-corpus graph is structurally attractive but empirically unvalidated. The claim here is that the primitive is the same, not that both halves have been shipped.

title: FAIR, by construction
text:
NoteStatus



Framing, mostly built. Findable, Accessible, and Interoperable hold today; Reusable is provenance-complete, with machine-readable licensing still on the roadmap.
What happened. We added a FAIR framing to the pitch and its data-fabric appendix. The interesting part is not that FAIR is a box funders like to see ticked. It is that the architecture already satisfies it, and for a reason most FAIR pitches miss.
Why it matters. FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) was written in 2016 for data that a machine can act on with minimal human help. It specified the properties and left the actor unnamed. Here the actor is the Research Agent. So this is not FAIR bolted on for legitimacy; it is the machine-actionability FAIR always assumed, with a concrete consumer attached.

title: FAIR, by construction
text:
The mapping
Each letter lines up with a mechanism the group already runs:

Findable. Self-advertising connectors and a searchable index, with persistent identifiers for every source and bundle.
Accessible. Open discovery, consumption gated on the visibility ladder. This is the nuance most people get wrong: FAIR’s “A” means open where possible, authenticated where necessary, and metadata reachable even when the data is not. It does not mean “everything is public.”
Interoperable. A standards-based RDF and DCAT graph, plus an open tool protocol (MCP), with typed links between claims.
Reusable. Portable, provenance-tagged bundles you can take with you. Corrections are filed alongside the original, not over it, so a later reader meets the trajectory, not a silently rewritten fact.

title: FAIR, by construction
text:
The honest part
Reusable is where we are not done. FAIR’s “R” wants a clear, machine-readable licence attached to every artifact, and that layer is still on the roadmap. It lives with the credentialed-source work: a connector to a licensed database must never let its output leak into a public bundle. So the accurate claim is Findable, Accessible, and Interoperable by construction today; Reusable is provenance-complete, with the licensing half still to build. Present tense means it runs; the licensing sentence is deliberately future.

title: FAIR, by construction
section: Why it is strategic, not decorative
text:
Why it is strategic, not decorative
Funders increasingly mandate FAIR data-management plans. “This makes your group’s outputs FAIR by construction, without a portal” is language a program officer recognizes on sight, and it complements the data-fabric framing cleanly: the fabric is the how, FAIR is the recognized principles the fabric serves.
The design detail behind all of this is in the wiki’s Discovery-Architecture page, which also treats decentralized identifiers as the “findable” persistent-identifier layer.

title: Knowledge, expertise, experience
text:
NoteStatus



Framing. A descriptive synthesis, and it names a tooling gap that is not yet built.


Thesis. An LLM-authored wiki can capture three separable things, and most systems capture only the first.
Knowledge is facts: what things are, how they relate. Expertise is judgment: which tradeoffs matter, what to prefer, what to distrust. Experience is the concrete cases behind the abstractions: what was tried, what worked, what failed, which dead ends are not worth revisiting. The three are separable but not hierarchical.
Vanilla RAG operates almost entirely at the knowledge layer. “Your files are searchable” is a knowledge claim. A reading list carries knowledge too. But a good graduate advisor adds expertise and experience on top of the reading list, and that is exactly the difference between a textbook and an advisor. A wiki that records all three is closer to the advisor. The wiki should ship compiled competence, not just searchable content.
The three-layer framing is a descriptive synthesis, not a term of art; it lines up with several existing frameworks at once (declarative versus procedural knowledge, the tacit-explicit distinction, case-based reasoning), which is why we resisted coining an acronym for it. It cuts across those axes rather than sitting neatly on any one.
The practical finding is that the experience layer is the thinnest, across every wiki we have looked at, relative to what it could hold. Failed approaches and grounded cases are exactly what a group’s tacit memory should preserve, and exactly what tends not to get written down. That is the largest tooling opportunity here: LLM-assisted authoring that captures expertise and experience as they happen, rather than hoping someone writes them up later. It does not exist yet.
This framing is orthogonal to the retrieval work. Retrieval decides whether the LLM finds the right pages. These three layers decide whether the right pages were worth finding.

title: A working connector against PAD
text:
NoteStatus



Built and demonstrated. This runs today; caveats are noted inline.


What happened. The loop was discover then authorize then consume then compose: read the federation index, use a GitHub credential to reach private PAD bundles, and answer with the live API plus the bundles’ validated model stats.
Why it matters. A ~180-line agent reads PAD’s own ontology and API and answers live questions, then composes with private federation bundles.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s PAD-Connector-POC. The public write-up with the card-image and Lasso-overlay scenario is the pad-example appendix.

title: A working plugin marketplace
text:
NoteStatus



Built and demonstrated. This runs today; caveats are noted inline.


What happened. It proves the marketplace-plugin delivery leg: discovery abstracts over whether a capability resolves to a connector you call or a plugin you install.
Why it matters. chrissweet/csweet-skills-marketplace packages two plugins from the AI_skills wiki, validated end to end with the Claude Code CLI.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s Skills-Marketplace-POC. Caveat: only install-from-local was exercised end to end.

title: Discovery, reframed around DIDs
text:
NoteStatus



Design. The intended approach, not yet implemented.


What happened. Decentralized identifiers make identity portable, the fourth leg of vendor-neutrality, and a DID document is itself part of the graph, so identity, description, and discovery live in one place.
Why it matters. Our discovery is a curated federation index, which contradicts the self-advertising-connector pitch. The target is decentralized self-description harvested into the graph.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s Discovery-Architecture. Nothing here is built yet; only the curated index and credentialed consumption run today.

title: How the LLM actually reads a wiki
text:
NoteStatus



Empirical observation. Grounded in real wikis. The caveat: predicate counts measure what got written, not what gets read at retrieval time.


Thesis. When an LLM answers a question against a wiki today, it navigates by name, not by graph.
Watch what actually happens. Two things do the work: the CLAUDE.md instructions that tell the agent to consult the wiki at all, and ordinary filesystem tools plus reading comprehension, read the index, grep for a matching filename, open the file. That is it. Navigation is name-based. [Coscientist-2023](Coscientist-2023) and the plain string Coscientist-2023 behave identically; the link syntax is decorative from the read path’s perspective. The LLM does not follow links as links, does not use typed edges as edges, and does not touch the reified RDF graph at all.
The uncomfortable implication: every improvement to link and edge quality improves a structure the LLM does not walk. Those improvements are real and worth making, they help humans, the extractor, and downstream pipelines, but they are invisible to the LLM’s current read path. In one wiki, typed edges saw zero adoption across 137 pages even though the machinery was sitting in the SCHEMA, and it changed nothing about how the LLM read that wiki.
None of this means the graph is useless. It means the graph is dark matter until something upstream of the LLM consumes it and injects the result into the LLM’s context. That something is a retrieval step: a random walk that reads the graph on the LLM’s behalf and hands it a reading list, at exactly the choice point where name-matching is blindest.
The honest scope: at the current scale (the largest of these wikis is around 130 pages) name-matched navigation is genuinely competitive. It breaks later, at larger scale, with shallow context assembly and lost cross-domain connections. The retrieval step is insurance against the scale we are heading toward, not a fix for a fire today.

title: From ask to full agentic systems
text:
NoteStatus



Provisional. Explicitly forward-looking; the framing should evolve as concrete agent work produces evidence.


Thesis. Agent systems are the consumers of the retrieval substrate. The vision provides the substrate; agents are the higher-order operators that call it.
At the light end is ask: a single shot. Extract seed concepts from the question, run a random walk over the wiki graph, take the top pages, synthesize an answer with citations. At the heavy end is a full agent system: plan, retrieve, act, and update the wiki as a side effect of working. The primitive that separates the two is action. Everything else, memory, retrieval, provenance, discipline, already exists in the template stack; the action primitive is what makes something an agent rather than a smart assistant with memory.
Three composition patterns fall out. Ask as a retrieval consumer. Wiki as agent memory, where the agent writes its findings and its log back as it goes. And corpus-scale retrieval, where an agent drops from the wiki down to the raw corpus when the wiki lacks coverage, then files a durable synthesis back up.
Three questions the vision does not yet answer. When should an agent trust the wiki versus fall back to the corpus (the cached-but-stale failure mode)? How does the action primitive interact with wiki correctness (provenance discipline matters more once an agent can write)? And what is the right ergonomic surface between the retrieval substrate and the orchestrator on top of it?
This one is deliberately provisional. The natural build progression looks like /wiki-ask, then /wiki-verify, then /wiki-plan, then multi-agent orchestration, but nothing on that list is committed to. The honest caution is the point: an agent that calls a bad retrieval tool still gets bad results. Agents raise the stakes for retrieval quality; they do not paper over retrieval limits.

title: Govern the substrate, leave the ecosystem open
text:
NoteStatus



Policy. A summary of a draft governance document (draft 0.1), not an experiment.


Thesis. The governance document’s normative authority is scoped to exactly one thing, the llm-wiki-template substrate. The wider ecosystem is left open and permissionless by design.
This is the point people miss. Governing the template and leaving the ecosystem open is the design choice, not an omission to be filled in later. The document names four parts of the ecosystem (substrate, plugins, bundles, federation), but that four-part taxonomy is a shared vocabulary for describing the ecosystem, not a mechanism for governing it. Anyone can build a connector, publish a bundle, or join the federation, with no gatekeeper.
The prescriptive core is the substrate-vs-plugin boundary. A proposal belongs in the substrate only if all four hold: it is agent-neutral, it touches the wiki format (SCHEMA, gate, extractor, ontology, installers), it is needed by nearly every derived project, and it has a small maintenance surface. Anything agent-specific, subtype-scoped, external-API-bound, or configuration-heavy is a plugin, in its own repo.
Two taxonomies do the descriptive work. Wikis self-classify into subtypes distinguished by what they are trying to be complete against: Experimental (the author’s own in-progress work), Review (a curated slice of a corpus), Landscape (a fast-moving external field, where coverage is a real success test), and Bounded (private working memory). Each names a producer-discipline level. Bundles split into Reference (a neutral retrieve-synthesize-cite protocol) and Pedagogical (a Socratic or worked-example protocol that must be embedded in the bundle’s own content to travel through ask).
The distinguishing line for a bundle is worth keeping: bundles are wikis, but not every wiki is a bundle. The property that makes one is intent to share and be queried by non-authors.

title: Lightweight structural graphs, and the entity-resolution trap
text:
NoteStatus



Argument. Grounded in predicate distributions from three real wikis (measured), not an A/B retrieval benchmark.


Thesis. Lightweight structural graphs plus lightweight extracted content, walked by a random walk, beat heavy content-graph engineering on the cost-benefit axis for experimental science.
There are three layers, not two. The structural layer is mechanical and unambiguous: chunks in documents, section hierarchies, parent chains, wikilinks. The lightweight extracted layer takes named entities, concepts, and citations at face value, keeping surface forms distinct with no canonicalization. The heavy layer is the expensive one: aggressive entity resolution plus formal, ontology-enforced typed assertions. The claim is that you should not build your retrieval loop around that third layer.
The reason is the entity-resolution trap. Heavy graphs fail two ways: under-merging, when “Obama” and “President Obama” stay separate nodes, and over-merging, when “Washington” collapses a person, a state, and a university into one. Resolution is unsolved for real corpora, and it fails worst on exactly the rare, context-disambiguated entities that scientists actually ask about. A structural or lightweight graph has no resolution step, so no resolution failure is possible.
And the coverage you give up is small. Research material is already organized around the structure the graph captures, and questions usually touch both a structural locus and a named entity, so a walk over the combined graph returns useful neighborhoods. The marginal gain from heavy typing is small next to its maintenance cost.
Heavy typing does pay off somewhere: bibliographic review wikis and fixed-ontology knowledge bases, where the material is objectively typeable. It does not pay off in experimental science, where forcing extends, supports, and criticizes onto provisional work manufactures a crispness the material does not have, and produces over-confident query results. The evidence for this is distributional, the predicate counts across three real wikis differ by exactly the amount their content shape predicts, and it is honest to say that is an argument from those counts, not a head-to-head retrieval benchmark.

title: Connectors are agents, not adapters
text:
NoteStatus



Design, with a working proof of concept. The general model is a design; one connector (PAD) has been built and run.


Thesis. A connector is not a static adapter. It is an agent.
A static adapter is a fixed function: same input, same call, same shape out. Real extraction is nothing like that. Reading a table out of a paper, reconciling a supplementary spreadsheet against the paper’s notation, negotiating a database’s quirks, normalizing to a schema, attaching provenance and a confidence, retrying when a source is malformed, that is open-ended, interpretive work. So a connector is an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, does the messy extraction, and files the result with its provenance. The researcher’s hub agent discovers and orchestrates a fleet of them.
Calling it an “agent” is the risky half, though, because an agent that guesses is worse than no agent. The reliability mechanism is a small ontology per source. An LLM reasoning over an explicit small ontology (what the entities are, how they relate, what the fields mean) is accurate; an LLM inferring that structure from raw, unlabelled responses is measurably less accurate and often needs a human in the loop. So the ontology is part of the connector contract: a connector is not finished when it can call the API, it is finished when the source is described well enough for an agent to reason over it without guessing.
One more load-bearing idea: discovery abstracts over delivery. The agent discovers a capability, say “graph extraction,” and invokes it without caring whether that resolves to a marketplace plugin it installs or a hosted service it calls. Delivery mode is an operations decision, never a user-facing one.
PAD (Paper Analytical Devices) is the first real candidate because it already self-describes, with both an OWL/RDF ontology and an OpenAPI spec. We read one to learn what it is and the other to learn how to call it, and built the connector.

title: When our own push protocol leaked conflict markers
text:
NoteStatus



Incident + fix. A real bug we hit and how it was resolved.


What happened. The nine CI scenarios never caught it because every test resolver succeeds; the fix checks the exit code and greps for markers before committing, plus a new failing-resolver scenario.
Why it matters. A real multi-writer collision: the semantic resolver reported failure and the wrapper committed the conflict markers anyway, because the resolver’s exit code was ignored.
Details. Tracked upstream as issue #94 on the llm-wiki-memory-template. Our instance is still on the pre-fix copy; the fix is to pull, not to re-derive.

title: The retrieval vision in one post
text:
NoteStatus



Framing. A reasoning trail, not a finished implementation. The corpus side is demonstrated; the wiki side is open work.


Thesis. A specific stance on graph-informed retrieval for LLM-maintained wikis and their source corpora, built on three claims.
First, the honest boundary: dense retrieval-augmented generation is well-suited to a broad class of applications, single-hop question answering, factual lookup, chat over documents, and it stays the right default there. The literature documents its limits most consistently where the data has alternate views the embedding cannot traverse: multi-hop reasoning, cross-document evidence chains, provenance. That is the band this vision is scoped to, scientific research, and nowhere else.
Claim one: distribution beats pattern-matching. A crisp query language like SPARQL returns empty when the structure it asks for is not there, which is the common case when typed edges are provisional and sparse. A random walk assigns some mass to every node, so sparse edges change where the mass lands rather than breaking the query, and it hands back a ranked neighborhood a language model can reason over.
Claim two: lightweight graphs carry most of the weight. Cheap, reliable structural edges (chunks in documents, section hierarchies, wikilinks, parent chains) plus lightweight extracted content (entity mentions, concept nodes, citation edges) add real semantic reach without paying the full cost of aggressive entity resolution and formal cross-document assertions, which is where heavy content graphs get brittle.
Claim three: one walk, two scales. The same random-walk primitive runs over the combined graph. At the wiki level it suggests which pages to read; at the corpus level it orders which chunks to extract. The walk math does not care whether an edge is structural or content-derived; it walks all of them, weighted by whatever the extractor emits.
What this is not: not a rejection of vanilla RAG, not a rejection of SPARQL for maintenance, and not a shipped system.

title: Reaching credentialed sources: the broker pattern
text:
NoteStatus



Partly built. The load-bearing shape is demonstrated; the general version is next.


What happened. It is the present-day form of the control-plane / data-plane split, and it is DID-ready at the same seam: swap the OAuth token for a verifiable credential later.
Why it matters. One rule: the LLM never touches the secret. A non-LLM broker holds the token and returns only data. SSH ControlMaster is a working in-house instance.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s Agent-Source-Auth. Honest limit: ControlMaster is a coarse, campus-local broker, not a scoped entitlement.

title: Locked in on nothing
text:
NoteStatus



Strategy. A position, not an artifact. The mechanisms it names (bundles, MCP, the template) are real; the market bet is an argument.


Thesis. Every vendor will hand your scientists a capable agent. None will give you a place to keep what the agent learns that you still control next year. That gap is the whole opportunity.
The frontier labs are all converging on the same shape: an agent, plus connectors, plus discovery. Committing to any one of their workbenches is portal lock-in reborn one layer up. The move that was right against bespoke portals is right against vendor workbenches: be the non-proprietary, LLM-agnostic layer that stays true no matter who wins.
That layer is neutral on three axes at once. A neutral agent: any LLM plugs in through the template’s overlays and skills, so adopting a better model is a new overlay, not a migration. Portable memory: your knowledge lives in an open bundle (markdown, frontmatter, and a reified RDF graph), so you can move to another solution and take it with you. Portable tools: connectors speak an open, cross-vendor protocol, so changing agents does not break them.
The ownership line is cleanest in CoALA’s terms. You rent the working tier from whatever platform you use today; you own the episodic, semantic, and procedural tiers, in a forkable template. Use whatever platform is best for capability, and keep your memory, your discipline, and your discovery in something you hold.
Two honesties. The tool-portability promise is exactly as strong as the open standard’s cross-vendor adoption; if the field fragments, the neutral template becomes the adapter that re-unifies it. And a circulated “LLM-wiki product landscape” named products we could not verify, so we do not assert them here.
Nobody else sells “locked in on nothing,” because nobody else’s business model allows it. Ours does, because it is a template, not a platform.

title: The architecture: template, instance, wiki, fabric
text:
NoteStatus



Accepted design. The interaction diagram is at version 1.0; its core pieces run today.


Thesis. The accepted interaction diagram separates three things earlier drafts collapsed into one.
The template is the shared, agent-neutral substrate: SCHEMA, verification gate, knowledge-graph extractor, ontology, installers. Every project reuses it. The instance is one instantiation of that template, a concrete project repo, the operational shell the user actually works in. The llm-wiki is the dedicated knowledge base created inside the instance, a separate git repo. It is the wiki, not the instance shell, that carries a subtype and is published as a bundle.
Why the separation matters, in one line: tools install into the instance; subtype and bundle-publishing are properties of the wiki. Collapsing them (as two earlier drafts did) hides where tools live and what actually gets shared.
The instance is the hub. The user and their LLM work in it. The template instantiates it and supplies built-in tools. Marketplaces install additional tools into it. The federation is a bidirectional connection, not a terminal: the instance’s wiki is published as a bundle, and the instance asks to consume other groups’ bundles. Marketplaces are to tools what the federation is to bundles: distribution-and-discovery layers under one visibility ladder (public, private, institutional, commercial), where neither layer enforces visibility, the artifact does.
A second rendering, the public flyer’s data-fabric styling, is a persuasion lens on the same system, and drawing it sharpened one thing: instantiation produces two coupled things sitting on the fabric, an agent and its llm-wiki memory, and that memory is reachable both by its own agent and via the federation. The fabric, not the template, is the substrate everything connects through.
Two earlier drafts were rejected along the way: one that put an abstract “consumer” at the top with pipelines flowing up to it, and one that drew the instance as a single box, hiding the wiki inside it.

title: The smallest experiment that would validate DIDs
text:
NoteStatus



Plan. Nothing here has been built or run yet.


What happened. Portability and one-graph are the hypotheses that would change our mind; a ledger extension (KERI over blockchain) is gated behind them.
Why it matters. Before any credential or harvesting work, mint did:web for two or three federation participants on GitHub Pages and test four falsifiable hypotheses.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s DID-Federation-Experiment. This is a plan, not a result.

title: Redrawing the architecture as a data fabric
text:
NoteStatus



Design. The intended approach, not yet implemented.


What happened. The redraw sharpened the model: the memory is reachable both by its own agent and via the federation, and the fabric, not the template, is the substrate everything connects through.
Why it matters. The template instantiates an agent and its llm-wiki memory onto a shared data fabric; boxes float above the fabric, tethered by a line and a dot, and the template has instantiate arrows to both.
Details. Drawn from the wiki’s Ecosystem-Architecture. The diagram is an inline SVG in the flyer; the source infographic is the data-fabric appendix.

title: When dense retrieval quietly fails at K=10
text:
NoteStatus



Built demonstration, with an honest boundary. The dense-retrieval failure numbers are measured on MuSiQue. The competing graph method’s win at 21k scale is not yet demonstrated; it is deferred on compute and is not projected here.


Thesis. Retrieval is not about answering a question. It is about assembling a context envelope, the local neighborhood of concepts, layout, and cross-document evidence the model needs. When the envelope is missing, the model cannot answer, even when the strictly correct chunk sits in the top-K.
The measured part is stark. On MuSiQue at 21,000 chunks, at the production cutoff of K equals 10, dense retrieval delivers the single direct-match chunk for a question and effectively nothing else. For a four-hop question, the model gets one gold chunk of the four it needs. The deepest four-hop chunk sits at a median rank of 1,796 out of 21,100, essentially never surfaced. Read as effective multi-hop retrieval, it collapses to 17 percent at two hops, and 0 percent at three and four. Those are measurements, not projections.
The framing that explains it: there are two kinds of hop. A reasoning hop lives on the question side and can be helped by decomposing the question. A retrieval hop lives on the index side, where the envelope actually is, and decomposition does not help you there. Multi-hop scientific questions are retrieval-hop-heavy by construction, which is why this whole line of work is scoped to scientific research rather than general question answering.
Now the boundary, stated as plainly as the site itself states it. Everything above is the dense-failure side, and it is measured. The claim that a graph-walk method fixes it at the same 21,000-chunk scale is not yet demonstrated. That comparison is incomplete, blocked on a distillation step estimated at about 300 dollars in compute, and the site does not project the walk’s win at scale. The dense method is shown to fail; the alternative is shown to be promising at small scale and honestly marked as unproven at large scale.
The line worth keeping: when the envelope is missing, the language model cannot answer, even when the strictly correct chunk is in the top-K.

title: WGRA Notes
text:
The running record behind WGRA: a wiki that sits between a research group’s sources and its AI agent as durable, shared, git-versioned memory. The main site holds the evergreen pitch and deep dives; these notes turn the project’s own log outward.
Two kinds of posts:

Field notes: dated, short, honest. What we built, what we learned, what we decided, what broke. Tagged field-notes.
Essays: longer arcs not already covered by a deep dive, the Research Agents strategy series (research-agents) and the retrieval foundations behind it (retrieval).

Each post carries a status: built, partly built, design, or plan. Present tense means it runs today.









Lightweight structural graphs, and the entity-resolution trap



retrieval



Cheap structural edges plus lightweight extracted content deliver most of the coverage; heavy content-graph engineering buys diminishing returns because entity resolution is fragile.





Jul 20, 2026







Govern the substrate, leave the ecosystem open



research-agents

governance



Governance authority is scoped to the template only; plugins, bundles, and federation are permissionless by design, with the taxonomies serving as shared vocabulary, not control.





Jul 20, 2026







Connectors are agents, not adapters



research-agents

connectors



Extraction is interpretive work, so a connector is an agent that wraps a source, declares a capability, and files provenance; a small ontology per source is what lets it reason instead of guess.





Jul 20, 2026







From ask to full agentic systems



research-agents



Consumers range from single-shot ask to multi-step plan/retrieve/act/update agents; the action primitive is what today’s template is missing.





Jul 20, 2026







How the LLM actually reads a wiki



retrieval



The LLM navigates by name-matched grep off the index, not by following links, so every edge-quality gain helps humans and extractors, not the LLM, until a retrieval step injects the graph.





Jul 20, 2026







The retrieval vision in one post



retrieval

The same walk at two scales



retrieval



One random-walk primitive suggests which wiki pages to read and orders which corpus chunks to extract: identical math, different graph.





Jul 20, 2026







When dense retrieval quietly fails at K=10



retrieval



On a 4-hop question over 21k chunks, dense retrieval delivers one gold chunk of four; the graph walk pins the bridge chunks it misses. Retrieval is envelope assembly, not question answering.





Jul 20, 2026







Why random-walk beats SPARQL for question answering



retrieval



SPARQL fails hard, returning empty, when structure is provisional; random-walk-with-restart degrades gracefully into ranked neighborhoods an LLM can reason over.

field-notes

tooling
